State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Executive Director
(916) 322-5662

General Counsel
322-5660

Enforcement
322-5901

Legal
322-6441

Statement of Economic Interest
322-6444

January 29, 1982


William P. Hopkins
City Attorney
Civic Center, Third Floor
200 S. Anaheim Blvd.
Anaheim, CA 92805

Re: Telephone Advice Request

Dear Mr. Hopkins:

Thank you for your telephone request for advice regarding
a decision coming before the Anaheim City Council on Tuesday,
February 2, 1982. In October 1981, you requested and received
written advice regarding a decision then pending before the
City Council. That decision involved the adoption/non-adoption
of a proposed "Occupancy Inspection" Ordinance. As a result
of that advice letter and the additional facts which you
were able to ascertain, you advised, and your clients agreed,
that three of the Council's five members were required to
disqualify themselves from participation in that decision
due to conflicts of interest. Following the Commission's
advice in the Hudson Opinion, 4 FPPC Op. 13 (7-7-007, February 7,
1978), the disqualified councilmembers drew lots, and Councilmember Roth was selected to sit and participate. He and the
two non-disqualified councilmembers decided to appoint a
citizens committee to study the problem of the deteriorating
housing stock and to propose possible solutions.

The advisory committee has completed its work and will
report back to the Council on Tuesday. It is entirely possible
that they may renew the proposal for an "Occupancy Inspection"
Ordinance. Your question is whether or not the same drawing
by lot would apply in that circumstance.

Assuming that the same three councilmembers are required
to disqualify themselves due to conflicts of interest as to
consideration of the same (or very similar) proposal, then
the council could utilize the same drawing by lot, since the
decision is really a continuation of the same issue. However,
each (all five) councilmember must analyze any pending
decision anew in light of then-current circumstances. If

William P. Hopkins
January 29, 1982
Page Two


there are changed circumstances (either as to their personal
financial interests or as to the proposed ordinance), which
alter the conclusion as to disqualification of any council-
member, then a new situation would exist requiring a fresh
look at the situation and, if appropriate, a new drawing by
lot.

     For instance, because of the intervening 3-1/2 month
period, a councilmember previously disqualified because of a
source of income during the then-preceding 12 month period
might no longer be disqualified.  Or a councilmember who
previously did not have a conflict might now have one.  If
only two councilmembers now have a conflict, then the Hudson
rule would not apply.  On the other hand, if four council-
members now have a conflict, then a new drawing would be
needed to select two of them to sit.

     As I discussed with you over the telephone, you should
carefully review the facts with each councilmember to determine
what advice should be given on Tuesday.

     Again, if I may be of further assistance, please do not
hesitate to call me at 916/322-5901.

                    Sincerely,

                    Robert E. Leidigh
                    Counsel
                    Legal Division

REL:km

CITY OF ANAHEIM,CALIFORNIA
CIVIC CENTER, 200 South Anaheim Boulevard, Third Floor
Anaheim, California 92805

Office of
CITY ATTORNEY

Telephone:
714/999-5169

January 27, 1982

Robert E. Leidigh, Esq.
Counsel, Legal Division
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

RE: Conflict of Interest - Occupancy Inspection Program

Dear Mr. Leidigh:

With reference to your letter of October 23, 1981, concerning the above-entitled subject, and our telephone conversations of January 27, 1982, I am attaching a copy of portions of the draft minutes prepared by the City Clerk of the Anaheim City Council meeting held on October 27, 1981.

As you will note after the discussion on a Home Occupancy Inspection Program, Councilman Bay moved that a Task Force be formed to (1) define the problem (2) recommend to the Council their ideas on the various alternate solutions to those problems (3) report back to the Council in 60 days.

I would appreciate receiving your informal opinion as to the following:

Is it necessary to have the City Council members who declared a conflict of interest before the City Council meeting of October 27, 1981, again declare a conflict of interest and conduct another drawing pursuant to the Hudson Opinion before hearing the report of the Task Force?

Can the City Council (all five members) receive the report of the Task Force to determine its contents before declaring any conflict of interest that may be involved based on the contents thereof.

Yours very truly,

William P. Hopkins
City Attorney

$2,462 and authorizing the Mayor and City Clerk to execute said agreement.

MOTION CARRIED.

RECESS: By general consent, the Council recessed to 7:00 P.M. (5:55 P.M.)

AFTER RECESS: Mayor Pro Tem Roth called the meeting to order, all Council

Members being present, with the exception of Councilman Seymour. (7:30 P.M.)

107: DISCUSSION - HOME OCCUPANCY INSPECTION PROGRAM: Request of the Patrick

Henry Neighborhood Council to discuss a Home Occupancy Inspection Program

(HOIP).

Mayor Pro Tem Roth welcomed those in attendance and asked the City Attorney to

first speak to the conflict of interest issue and to explain the Fair

Political Practices Commission (FPPC) ruling on the matter.

City Attorney William Hopkins reported the opinion his office rendered

concerning the conflict of interest of Mayor Seymour and Mayor Pro Tem Roth

was confirmed by a letter received yesterday from the FPPC. He had also

received a decision concerning Councilman Overholt's position and, based on

information reviewed today, it was determined that Councilman Overholt had

clients in the real estate industry who would be affected by the type of

decision that would be involved in an occupancy inspection program.

Therefore, based on the opinion of the FPPC and the opinion of his office,

there were three members of the Council who would not be able to participate

in the proceeding tonight or any subsequent vote that might arise on occupancy

City Hall, Anaheim, California - COUNCIL MINUTES - October 27, 1981, 1:30 P.M.

inspection. The further opinion of the FPPC, when three members of a

five-member Council were disqualified, the rule of legal necessity called for

a drawing to determined which of the three members would be permitted to

participate despite the fact that there was a disqualification. Based on that

opinion, a drawing was held this afternoon at the Council meeting and Mayor

Pro Tem Roth was the selected Council Member to participate in order to make

the quorum.


The Pro Tem then opened the public discussion first to the Patrick Henry

Neighborhood Council who requested the meeting proposing that the City adopt

an ordinance similar to that in force in Pasadena (copy of the City of

Pasadena Housing and Community Development Department Occupancy Inspection

Program--revised October 13, 1980--on file in the City Clerk's office) to be

followed by public input from those in favor of such a program.


Before continuing, Councilman Overholt stated that he was leaving the rostrum

but would remain in the room and not participate in any deliberation.


Mrs. Sheri Pignone, Chairperson, Patrick Henry Neighborhood Council, 1181

Crown Street, explained their purpose was to recommend that the Council adopt

an occupancy certificate program which the Patrick Henry Neighborhood Council

(PHNC) along with other neighborhood councils believed essential for quality

housing in the City. She then submitted a folder of material to the Council

(on file in the City Clerk's office) containing letters supporting such a

City Hall, Los Angeles, California - COUNCIL MINUTES - October 27, 1981, 1:30 P.M.

In summation, Mrs. Pignone stated it seemed that everyone was in agreement
that something should be done but the method was in question. In their
judgement, all methods had been tried. Several people stated there were codes
and ordinances on the books. However, she emphasized that they had not been
effective. The proposed program was not another law but the tool to enforce
the Codes and Ordinances already in existence.


Testimony was then given by Len Reitz, the Housing Code Enforcement Officer,
whose responsibility covered the four target areas but in answer to complaints
only. He emphasized that he could use assistance especially by someone who
was bi-lingual.


MOTION: After further discussion, Councilman Bay moved that a Task Force be
formed as suggested by Mr. Maggi with the make-up as follows: A bi-lingual
Housing Coordinator, one representative from the Patrick Henry Neighborhood
Council, one owner from the area, a representative from the Apartment Owners
Association with no financial interest in the area, a representative from the
Chamber of Commerce, one tenant from the area, a representative from the
City's legal staff, a member from the Housing Department of the City, one
member at large appointed by the City Manager, a representative from the
County Health Department.


Before further action was taken, Patricia Clancy suggested that the Task Force
also include a representative from each of the other three neighborhood

councils as well; City Manager Talley suggested that representatives from City

staff be advisory only rather than connoting voting rights; Councilman Roth

suggested that a representative from the Realty Board be continued;

Councilwoman Kaywood suggested that there be another owner from the area, two

instead of one.


Councilman Bay amended his motion as follows: That a task force be formed as

follows: City Staff Support (Advisory), Bilingual Housing Coordinator (1),

Representative from Legal Staff (1), Representative from Housing Department

Staff (1), City Manager appointee (1), Representative from County Health

Department (1). Balance of Task Force (with voting rights), Representatives

from the Four Neighborhood Council (1 from each Council), Two Apartment Owners

in the area, One representative from the Orange County Apartment Owner's

Association with no financial interest in the area, One representative from

the Chamber of Commerce, One tenant in the area, One representative from the

Anaheim Board of Realtors.


Councilman Roth seconded the motion.


Before a vote was taken, Councilman Bay clarified that the apartment owners,

the two within the 94 unit Robin, Wren, Bluejay, Chevy Chase area. The task

force was to make a study, not a long, formal detailed study, but to come back

to the Council with the definition and scope of the problem in the area.


A vote was then taken on the foregoing motion. Councilman Seymour was

City Hall, Anaheim, California - COUNCIL MINUTES - October 27, 1981, 1:30 P.M.

MOTION: Councilman Bay the moved that the Task Force (1) define the problem,

(2) recommend to the Council their ideas on various alternate solutions to

those problems, (3) report back to the Council in 60 days. Councilman Seymour

was absent. Councilman Overholt abstained. MOTION CARRIED.

sections.question: Your question is whether or not the same drawing by lot would apply in that circumstance.
sections.conclusion: Assuming that the same three councilmembers are required to disqualify themselves due to conflicts of interest as to consideration of the same (or very similar) proposal, then the council could utilize the same drawing by lot, since the decision is really a continuation of the same issue. However, each (all five) councilmember must analyze any pending decision anew in light of then-current circumstances. If there are changed circumstances (either as to their personal financial interests or as to the proposed ordinance), which alter the conclusion as to disqualification of any councilmember, then a new situation would exist requiring a fresh look at the situation and, if appropriate, a new drawing by lot.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the Anaheim City Council can use the same drawing by lot procedure (from the previous October 1981 decision regarding the Occupancy Inspection Ordinance) when the citizens committee reports back with potentially renewed or similar proposals.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that the same drawing by lot procedure may be used if the same three councilmembers have conflicts on the same or similar proposal, as it constitutes a continuation of the same issue. However, each councilmember must reassess their conflicts of interest based on current circumstances; if any councilmember's conflict status has changed due to elapsed time or modified ordinance provisions, a fresh analysis and potentially new drawing would be required.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the FPPC response letter (January 29, 1982) and the City Attorney's incoming request/supporting documents (January 27, 1982) and October 27, 1981 council minutes. The structured extraction focuses on the FPPC advice letter (Document ID 82A088) as the primary document. Question and conclusion sections are direct quotes from the letter.

QUESTION: Your question is whether or not the same drawing by lot would apply in that circumstance.

CONCLUSION: Assuming that the same three councilmembers are required to disqualify themselves due to conflicts of interest as to consideration of the same (or very similar) proposal, then the council could utilize the same drawing by lot, since the decision is really a continuation of the same issue. However, each (all five) councilmember must analyze any pending decision anew in light of then-current circumstances. If there are changed circumstances (either as to their personal financial interests or as to the proposed ordinance), which alter the conclusion as to disqualification of any councilmember, then a new situation would exist requiring a fresh look at the situation and, if appropriate, a new drawing by lot.

embedding.summary: FPPC counsel responds to the City Attorney of Anaheim regarding whether the same drawing by lot procedure used in October 1981 can be applied when a citizens committee reports back on the Occupancy Inspection Ordinance proposal, advising that while the same procedure may apply if circumstances remain unchanged, each councilmember must reanalyze potential conflicts based on current conditions.
